data.title: Market And Economic Insights From March 2025
data.description:
Introduction

Welcome to the SA’s The Macro Brief! This official SA Profile will highlight our analysts' latest economic and market analysis to help investors gauge the ever-volatile financial landscape through various recurring series.

The economy is the foundation of financial markets, influencing everything from corporate earnings and consumer spending to central bank policy and inflation. Understanding economic trends, policy decisions, and sector activity can be critical when assessing market opportunities and making informed investment decisions.

Check out these must-reads from March…

March’s Trending Themes

Trump, Tariffs, Trade Wars Part III

Trump Cares About The Stock Market More Than You Think | Lawrence Fuller | 3/12/25

“President Trump's erratic trade policies and contradictory statements have caused significant market volatility and investor uncertainty.”

The Tariff Tantrum Has Created A Buying Opportunity | Victor Dergunov | 3/17/25

"The market correction may be nearing its bottom, presenting a potential buying opportunity for high-quality stocks that have seen significant declines."

Trump's April 2 Tariffs: Everything You Need To Know | Danil Kolyako | 3/31/25

"The core idea of reciprocal tariffs is to impose U.S. tariffs on imports from a country equivalent to the tariffs that the country imposes on U.S. exports. President Trump stated on March 30th that these tariffs would initially apply to all countries."

What To Fear From Tariffs | MarketGauge | 3/31/25

"Regardless of what you believe the long-run implications of the Trump tariff ideology will be, the impact over the last several months has been an increase in bearish sentiment about the future trends in inflation and growth."

Market Volatility & Corrections

SPY: Be Like Berkshire And Buffett And Get Your House In Order For The Reckoning To Come | Jim Sloan | 3/10/25

"The stock market is likely to experience low returns between 2025 and 2035, potentially becoming a "lost decade" for investors with high valuation as a major underlying cause."

The Stock Market Correction Is Likely Just Getting Started - Where I'm Investing Instead | Samuel Smith | 3/15/25

“While some may view this sharp pullback in the S&P 500 as an opportunity to buy the dip, I do not think that the market offers even close to a compelling risk-reward valuation at these levels.”

The Market's Worst Fears Just Became Reality | Mott Capital Management | 3/30/25

"A flood of data is coming, and multiple job and PMI reports may reveal the market’s worst fear—a troubling combination of rising prices and weakening job growth."

S&P 500: Ready For A Capitulation - Week Starting 31st March (Technical Analysis) | Andrew McElroy | 3/30/25

"Wednesday's tariff announcement is a concern, but I am prepared to buy a capitulation move as President Trump is likely to walk back some of the initial measures."

Recession Fears Reemerge

The Fed Has A Big Problem - And No Clear Solution | Mott Capital Management | 3/16/25

“The Fed is in a very tough position. It's no longer in charge, ceding that control to the Trump administration, which seems to change tariffs and geopolitical policy daily.”

SPY: The Recessionary Bear Market With Bubble Burst Is In-Progress | Damir Tokic | 3/26/25

“Earnings estimates for the Value stocks have collapsed over the last three months, as analysts are starting to incorporate recessionary views.”

This Indicator Predicts A Recession Soon (It Has Been Accurate For Nearly 30 Years) | Eugenio Catone | 3/31/25

“Despite frequent recession fears, the stock market has remained resilient, and traditional indicators like the yield curve inversion and Sahm Rule have proven unreliable recently.”

Why The 'Trump Recession' May Not Materialize | Victor Dergunov | 3/31/25

“Despite recent market volatility and fears of recession and stagflation, I believe the market is overreacting and not weighing long-term fundamentals accurately.”

S&P 500 Plunged In March, Recession Fears Rising | Mike Zaccardi, CFA, CMT | 3/31/25

“The S&P 500 fell for a second straight month in March, posting its first down quarter since Q3 2023. Now trading at about 20x earnings, recession fears seem to grow each passing week.”

Conclusion

We hope our analysts' insights serve as a valuable financial compass for investors seeking to make informed investment decisions.

Thanks for reading. Tell us what you think by commenting below! Where do you agree? Where do you think markets and the economy are headed in Q2? If you'd like to see more, follow this account.

Please let us know if you have any suggestions you’d like to see added to this series and any ideas for new additional recurring series. We're always open to your valuable feedback.

To stay current on the latest economic and market events coming up, subscribe to Wall Street Breakfast, Seeking Alpha's flagship daily business news summary.

data.title: British American Tobacco Needs Time To Consolidate (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
BTI stock enters consolidation

My last analysis on British American Tobacco (BTI) was published more than a month ago, in February 2025. That analysis, entitled "British American: Total Shareholder Yield Justifies Holding Despite Slow Growth," reviewed its Q4 earnings with a focus on its total shareholder yield. I argued that the stock is a solid hold on the following considerations:

British American Tobacco p.l.c.'s Q4 and FY 2024 earnings report indicates slow growth ahead due to various headwinds. But its total shareholder yield still makes it a solid hold. The stock's current dividend yield is about 7.8%. Share buyback program can push the total shareholder yield to nearly 9%. I also expect its strong cash generation to sustain a high total shareholder yield in the years to come.

Since then, there have been a few new issues evolving around BTI stock. In the remainder of this article, I will concentrate on the top 2 on my list: Its latest technical trading patterns and also the potential of new tariff rates. An examination of these issues has led me to conclude that BTI is entering a consolidation phase in the near term. As such, I don't see an obvious bias in its stock price movements and reiterate my hold rating on the stock.

BTI stock: Technical trading patterns

BTI's stock price has enjoyed a nice rally between January and mid-February, as you can see from the following chart. However, its latest trading pattern signals an impending consolidation phase for several reasons. First, after a strong surge to a high of ~$42 in mid-February, the stock has failed to establish new highs, instead fluctuating around the $41 mark, slightly above its 20-day moving average of $40.32. Second, I want to draw your attention to the price-volume bar highlighted by the green rectangle boxes below. This price range has been the trading window that has accumulated the heaviest volumes lately. Thus, this price range (of around $35 to $36) represents a key support level. BTI's current stock price has risen above this support level too far and too quickly. I believe it will take time for the large number of traders participating in the $35 to $36 price range to be replaced by more aggressive ones (or turn into more aggressive ones themselves).

The next set of charts show BTI's 50-2 Bollinger bands and Relative Strength Index - RSI - information, and I see a few indicators for an impending consolidation phase here too. The stock price breached the upper Bollinger Band in mid-February, but has pulled back to be squarely within the bands as seen, indicating increasing resistance for another breakout attempt. Furthermore, note that RSI is currently at 63.65, while still indicating some buying pressure, has subdued substantially from the overbought readings (almost approaching 90) observed in early February.

But above all else, the most important sign on this chart in my view is the narrowing/flattening of the Bollinger Bandwidth - BB. At my last writing, the low and upper BB were about $33 and $41, respectively, resulting in a bandwidth of ~$8. Now, the low and upper BB have flattened to ~$36 to $42, resulting in a bandwidth of about $6 only. Such a flattened/flattening BB further reinforces the likelihood of sideways movement in my model.

BTI stock: Valuation and earnings outlook

In terms of fundamentals, my assessment is that the stock is currently attractive valued in absolute terms but trades around a fair price in relative terms. To wit, as you can see from the following chart, BTI receives an overall valuation grade of "A" for good reason. The forward P/E ratios, both non-GAAP and GAAP, are significantly lower than the sector median. The forward non-GAAP P/E for BTI is only 9.04, a substantial 44.57% below the sector median of 16.31. However, when compared to its own five-year averages, the current P/E ratios suggest roughly fair valuation (with a discount/premium in my range of around -6% to 9% as seen).

In the case of BTI, I believe the valuation comparison to its own track record is more relevant due to its growth prospects. As a reflection, the consensus estimate for its EPS (shown in the next chart) shows a moderate growth curve ahead. To wit, its FY 2025 EPS is projected to be $4.55 with year-over-year growth of 1.05% only, significantly below the inflation escalator. The EPS is projected to increase to $4.77 by FY 2026 and $5.08 in 2027, reflecting a moderate year-over-year growth rates in the 5% to 6% range.

And I indeed see a mix of headwinds and tailwinds for its EPS growth in the years to come, as detailed next.

BTI stock: Other risks and final thoughts

First and foremost, I look for global tobacco industry volumes to continue its downward trend in the next few years, pressuring BTI traditional combustible products. On the positive side, I expect the New Categories Products to offset the decline to some degree. These products include vapor, tobacco heating, and modern oral goods, which have been showing promise. In the meantime, BTI has been pressured by unfavorable foreign exchange rates, and I think these headwinds are very likely to remain in the near future. The largest immediate uncertainty on my radar is the impact of high U.S. tariffs. The impact can be complex and nonlinear. But my model shows more negative consequences than positive ones. Tariffs on imported tobacco products or related materials would directly increase BTI's costs. As such, BTI could become less competitive compared to domestic U.S. tobacco companies. Above all else, I expect higher tariffs to cause the price of other products to rise, thus reducing consumers' discretionary spending and their demand for BTI's products.

Before closing, I feel an analysis on BTI would not be complete without a look at its dividend. And here again the picture I'm seeing is mixed. The next chart depicts its dividend yield over the past five years. Currently, the yield stands at 7.29%. The good news is that a 7%-plus yield provides a substantial current income and also downside protection. However, the bad news is that it's below its historical average of 7.80%.

All told, my verdict is that, under current conditions, the positives are roughly in balance with the negatives. To recap, BTI's technical trading signals since my last writing have led me to conclude that the stock needs time to consolidate. The potential of new tariff rates adds further uncertainties to its business fundamentals. Although for long-term shareholders, I feel positive about the progress of its new products, its fair P/E, and also generous dividend payouts. I've learned from experiences that business fundamentals determine long-term trends, while technical trading largely determines near term price behavior. That's why in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) monitors both to isolate risks and maximum alpha. In the case of BTI, I'm currently sensing a mixed signal and thus reiterate my Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CG Oncology Looks Attractive At Current Prices (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I covered CG Oncology, Inc. (NASDAQ:CGON) in August when I said I thought it had promising data but looked expensive. The stock is down 41% since then, prompting me to take another look at it.

Background

CGON develops backbone bladder-sparing therapies for bladder cancer. The lead asset is cretostimogene grenadenorepvec, an intravesically delivered oncolytic immunotherapy. It is a genetically modified adenovirus that is

“designed to preferentially replicate in retinoblastoma (Rb) gene pathway-defective cells present in the majority of urothelial carcinomas and trigger an anti-tumor immune response.”

Two modifications to the viral construct include a promoter that helps it selectively replicate in diseased cells while sparing healthy cells and a GM-CSF gene that increases its potency. It works by directly killing tumor cells, and then indirectly when antigens released during lysis trigger a systemic immune response.

In that earlier coverage, we noted that, in a phase 3 monotherapy study in NMIBC or high-risk non-muscle invasive bladder cancer refractory to Bacillus Calmette-Guerin, creto achieved a 75.2% complete response at any time in 105 patients. The responses were durable, with ~40% of these patients maintaining their CR at 12 months. A phase 2 study in combination with Keytruda (pembrolizumab) also saw an 82.8% CR at any time.

This data is highly competitive. Keytruda monotherapy was approved at just 41% CR rate. JNJ’s TAR-200 had 77% CR in a phase 2 trial, while ImmunityBio, Inc.'s (IBRX) Anktiva, approved for NMIBC, saw a 62% CR rate in a phase 3 trial.

We also discussed the target market in my earlier article, which we pegged at multiple billions of dollars given the sizable patient population. We will discuss this in more detail later.

Recent updates

In November, data from a phase 1 cisplatin-ineligible MIBC trial of creto combined with nivolumab was published in Nature Medicine. No dose-limiting toxicity and no grade 3 or higher treatment-related adverse events (TRAE) were seen in the trial. A CR rate of 41.2% was seen in this difficult-to-treat population, which is much higher than what is seen with nivolumab monotherapy. Recurrence-free survival rate at 1 year was 70.4%. The company expects a BLA submission in the second half of 2025.

In a late-breaking abstract presented at the Society of Urologic Oncology (SUO) in December for the BOND-003 trial, data was further updated to show a 74.5% CR rate. At 27 months, mDOR or Median duration of response had not been reached. Adverse events were grade 2 or below.

Valuation

I created a basic DCF valuation of CGON based on the potential for creto in NMIBC alone. My assumptions were:-

  • Patients - 70,000 total annual incidence, based on the two facts - a) 75% of newly diagnosed bladder cancer patients have NMIBC, and b) 83,000 patients diagnosed in 2024. This assumption does not consider an annual increase in the number of patients.
  • Price - $300,000 per patient per year based on Anktiva price of $437,440 and Keytruda price of $192,700 (WAC).
  • Terminal year - 10 years given their patents start expiring by 2036.

Other assumptions were industry standards:

COGS (% of revenue) 30%.

R&D + SG&A (Annual) $100mn.

Tax Rate 21%.

WACC 10%.

Terminal Growth Rate 3%.

Given the competition and that the drug is not yet approved, I discounted everything by 50%, just to be safe. I assumed a market penetration rate of 1%, simply doubling every year. This is the crux of the analysis because whether the company can achieve this rate is based on many factors not immediately visible to us now.

Given these assumptions, I get an enterprise value of the company based on this molecule in NMIBC alone at around $5.3bn. Given the 76mn shares outstanding, that gives me a price target of around $69. This is about 3x the current price, a number I can live with. This is also similar to Roth Capital’s price target of $65.

Financials

CGON has a market cap of $1.87bn and a cash balance of $742mn. They did a secondary offering in December, with gross proceeds of $238mn to CGON. R&D expenses were $26.8 million for the fourth quarter of 2024, while G&A expenses were $11.7 million, giving a cash burn of $38.5 million. Their CFO was ($20.7mn) and their capex was ($0.2mn), which gives them an FCF of -$20.9mn and a cash runway of over 35 quarters. According to the company, their cash runway extends to the first half of 2038, which is around 12 quarters. They probably assume higher expenses before approval and during commercialization.

Risks

The NMIBC market has some competition, and any valuation model is blinded by its own assumptions about how much market penetration a given molecule can achieve. While one expects that market penetration will be a simple function of the efficacy of a molecule, in reality, it doesn’t work like that. A big pharma with a poor molecule but strong expertise in marketing and sales can beat a small pharma with no such expertise but with a great molecule. So there’s always that risk.

Bottom Line

Given its high cash position, positive competitive positioning of creto in NMIBC, and also considering the basic valuation I did here, I will rate CGON a buy at current prices.

data.title: Red Cat: Overlooked Drone Opportunity
data.description:
The drone market has a huge future in the military, but Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:RCAT) has seen the stock slip to the recent lows. The drone technology company is guiding to a huge revenue ramp up in 2025 due to new contracts, with the potential for substantial growth in 2026. My investment thesis is even more Bullish, with the stock down over 50% to only $6.

Tough Transition

As with a lot of small companies, generally in the pre-revenue realm, current results are difficult to analyze. Red Cat reported the following Q4 results:

The worse part is the timeliness issues with the earnings reports for these developing companies. Red Cat didn't report Q4 2024 results until March 31, which can confuse the market expecting a big jump in revenues.

The company only reported transition period revenue of $4.85 million, while the market wants to look at the Q2/Q3 results for the period starting now. Sometimes the market mindset gets warped with the discussion on December quarter numbers versus the current June quarter.

With April just starting, the market wants to focus on the SRR ramp and the other revenue opportunities in 2025. Red Cat plans to host an Investor Day Town Hall in a couple of weeks to include the CFO after having a limited earnings call following 2024 results.

Over the 8-month transition period, Red Cat burned nearly $21 million from operations. The company burned about $2.5 million per quarter, but the big question is the cash burn once the business enters production mode with positive gross margins.

Big Future

Red Cat is now full speed ahead on the guidance to generate 2025 revenues of at least $80 million, with the potential for jumping up to $120 million. The U.S. Army's SRR is the only amount up in the air as to how much revenue the company obtains this year beyond the initial $25 million.

The key to the story is that the expectation is for a huge jump in sales starting in Q3/Q4, but the numbers only soar into 2026. The military market for drones is only forecast at $16 billion last year, but Fortune Business Insights has the market reaching a $47 billion market opportunity by 2032 for a 13% CAGR.

The work with Palantir Technologies (PLTR) helps boost the capabilities of the drone with the addition of visual navigation. The military opportunity is possibly far above the analyst estimates, with Ukraine targeting annual drone production at 4 million units after starting 2024 with monthly contracts for 500K.

For now, analyst estimates as for the following quarterly revenues:

  • Q1 2025 - $2M
  • Q2 2025 - $7M
  • Q3 2025 - $31M
  • Q4 2025 - $40M

Even this analyst estimate only assumes the company generates the low-end revenue total for 2025 of $80 million. The opportunity is for Red Cat to produce sales 50% above these estimates.

Red Cat ended the year with cash of $9 million and has an additional $15 million in debt financing raised in February. The company suggests the business has enough cash to reach the production targets for 2025, but investors should expect some capital raises on stock rallies with the $100 million mixed securities shelf.

Though, the business has filed for $58 million in debt financing from the DoD Office of Strategic Capital. Also, Red Cat has the potential for additional funding for drone swarming technology and next-gen products.

At the Investor Day, the company forecasts hardware gross margins hitting around 40% after 4 quarters of the new manufacturing facility with the Black Widow sales. On the prior earnings call, the CEO confirmed the Palantir deal would add ~80% gross margins for the software as follows:

Now, add Palantir software that can do visual navigation, which is very difficult to do with real-time updates, combined with artificial intelligence from someone that's been collecting objects and data from machine learning for 20 years in wars. That is very high margin revenue. So if you throw some number, I'm not going to give a number on the software, you can do it. $10,000, $20,000 per drone of software that can do those things. Their margins are up near 80%, 90%. So you put those software margins add on to our 50% gross margins, you can dramatically increase the margins going forward, which hopefully gets us to cash flow profitable -- profitability quicker.

The big opportunity is to replace sales by Chinese drone make DJI with and estimated up to $1 billion in drone sales in the U.S. opening up the door for Red Cat. The Countering CCP Drones ACT aims to block Chinese drones for national security reasons.

Any sales for this DJI replacement opportunity, Palantir software upgrades and international military sales aren't even included in the revenue estimates for 2025. The revenue opportunity is clearly far above the $100 million target for this year.

The stock currently has a market cap of ~$600 million. Red Cat trades at about 6x sales estimates and quickly gets very cheap on any sign the drone company can vastly grow sales in 2026.

The biggest risk to the stock story is the lack of capital. Red Cat being forced to raise additional capital on any stock weakness would be disastrous for shareholders.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Red Cat is cheap after the selloff. The drone stock faces a volatile period with the transition from minimal sales to full production mode, while limited capital could constrain operations.

Investors should use the recent weakness to load up on Red Cat.

data.title: DPG Vs. DNP: A Swap Could Make Sense
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

DNP Select Income Fund (DNP) is a highly popular closed-end fund for income investors. Its greatest boast is having never cut its regular distribution since the fund's inception. For this fund, that goes back to 1987 and that is quite a feat considering the number of economic environments the fund has found itself in. There are only a handful of funds that can even brag about never cutting their distribution, with inception going back to just before the Global Financial Crisis.

However, while investors highly value that fund for its consistent monthly distribution, the fund actually has an ugly cousin that is lesser known. That would be the Duff & Phelps Utility and Infrastructure Fund (NYSE:DPG). DNP had pretty consistently traded at an elevated premium, but that has been much more modest these days. The fund still trades at a premium, but it isn't as high as it historically has been.

For some context, on Seeking Alpha's quote page for each fund, DNP has 9.45k followers, and DPG has just 3.68k. DPG has only been around since 2011—and just a couple of years ago, they cut their distribution. Before the distribution cut, DPG had also been able to trade at a pretty elevated premium as well. However, for most of its life, it has been trading at a fairly wide discount.

With that being said, I believe that DPG is the better-looking fund of the two—and I fully expect there will be some major pushback. The main argument is going to be that DNP has never cut its distribution historically. However, on a valuation and performance basis, and even distribution safety going forward, DPG is the winner. That is what I'm going to attempt to highlight today.

Similar Performance

When looking back since the inception of DPG in terms of a performance comparison between the two, DNP has overwhelmingly dominated in terms of performance. It isn't even closer on a total share price return basis, but it is even by a wider margin in terms of total NAV returns.

However, the 'since inception' period isn't as elevated because DPG changed its investment policy and name.

Effective November 22, 2019, the name of Duff & Phelps Global Utility Income Fund Inc. was changed to Duff & Phelps Utility and Infrastructure Fund Inc.

With this change, they dropped more of the global emphasis in favor of making the policy more similar to DNP. Clearly, they were probably looking at the performance difference and knew something had to change.

When looking at performance since January 1, 2020 (we are giving DPG some time from the actual effective date), we can see a big change. DPG actually outperformed on a total share price basis by a significant margin, while at the same time, the total NAV returns were quite similar. To be fair, both of these funds took an absolute beating during the Covid sell-off. These are both leveraged funds, so that's what we should expect.

In looking at the last three years of performance, DPG once again comes out on top in terms of total share price returns and even slightly in terms of total NAV returns this time too.

Over the last year, things have started to turn more dramatically in favor of DPG.

Of course, this doesn't mean that DPG will continue to outperform going forward. They now have similar investment policies, but they still are a bit different with DPG still retaining some international leaning. Over lengthier periods of time, we should assume these funds can and will likely start to drift in terms of performance. However, since the fund's investment policy change and over the last three years, the underlying performance as measured by total NAV returns have been quite highly correlated.

Valuation Favors DPG

Given that we don't know future performance for sure and anything can happen going forward, that's where valuation can play a big role. Here, on an absolute basis, the clear winner is DPG by a wide margin.

On a relative basis, that's where it gets a bit more tricky as it suggests both funds are actually undervalued. We are looking at the last five years because that's measuring after DPG changed its name and investment policy. For DPG, we are seeing about a 7 point difference between its average discount compared to its current discount. For DNP, the differential is almost 12 points when looking at its average discount compared to its current discount.

Further, DPG cut its distribution, so investors dumped the fund to a massive discount from a premium in a very short period of time. The argument could then be made that DNP is the favorite in terms of valuation on a relative basis. While I do believe that DNP is attractive at this time for that reason, I would still favor DPG on this front. If we get similar performance going forward as we saw DPG holding up and even outperforming DNP now, I'd rather buy assets at a discount rather than a premium.

The other potential catalyst is if an activist investor ever got involved with DPG, they could push for changes to the fund. They could push for a distribution increase or even potentially merge DPG into DNP. In that scenario, DPG investors would become DNP investors at the premium level. That said, usually, upon an announcement of those sorts of moves, the funds would meet somewhere in the middle—which means that DPG would still likely benefit. That's not a consideration at the moment, at least not something that is publicly known, but it is always a possibility.

Distribution

The biggest argument I think that a swap between DNP to DPG would face is the fact that DPG cut its distribution when DNP hasn't.

DNP has also paid a monthly distribution consistently when DPG only more recently switched to a monthly payout. This change was likely put into place to make it more appealing to income-focused investors. When they made this change, they kept the same quarterly equivalent of $0.21 but just divided it into thirds to pay at a rate of $0.07 per month. Some could otherwise see this DPG distribution history chart below and mistakenly think they made another cut.

The second argument that is likely going to be presented by folks is the distribution rate itself. DPG pays a modest 6.97% share price distribution yield, and DNP is at 8.16%. That's a full 1 point differential in favor of DNP.

I'd just point out that the lower distribution rate for DPG means it is likely more sustainable and, therefore, relatively safer. Going back to the premium versus discount again, the NAV distribution rate for DNP comes to 8.42%. Due to the slight premium, the fund actually has to earn more in its portfolio than investors even receive.

For DPG, the NAV distribution rate comes to 6.23%. This means that DNP investors may enjoy a 1.19 point higher yield, but the fund has to earn a 2.19 point differential to support the payout. Now, I don't believe that DNP is likely to cut its distribution, but if we are in a prolonged bear market, DNP might be in a position where they had to before DPG.

That said, a caveat to consider here is that the operating expenses for DPG came to 1.61% in its last annual report. That can be compared to 1.03% for DNP. That brings the difference needed to support the distribution a bit closer, since DPG's portfolio needs to earn a bit more to cover the higher expenses.

Risks

There are three main risks

  • A basic risk of making a swap here would be that DNP continues to trade at an elevated premium and rises to its historical average level. DPG simultaneously could continue to sag with a double-digit discount and never move. It's also even possible that DPG goes to an even wider discount. Therefore, a swap between DNP to DPG would result in lower total returns.
  • I already mentioned it, but I wanted to reiterate that another risk is that these funds are not exactly identical. The performance since DPG's investment policy change means that it has moved more similar to DNP, but they still have some differences. If the underlying portfolios start to make more meaningful divergences, it could favor DNP, and DPG could once again, under this scenario, underperform—muting the potential benefits of swapping in terms of the valuation differential.
  • Finally, DNP is a much larger fund with roughly $4.526 billion in total managed assets compared to DPG's $663 million. That can create a significant difference in terms of liquidity with a higher average trading volume for DNP by a meaningful degree. The average number of shares traded for DPG is ~84.5k or roughly $1.022 million. For DNP, we see the average trading volume at ~721.7k or over $6.9 million trading daily.

Conclusion

DNP has a long history of steady monthly distributions that income investors covet. DPG's history isn't as attractive given the distribution cut, and they were paying a quarterly distribution, only more recently switching to a monthly payout.

However, that isn't always necessarily the best gauge when it comes to making investing decisions. In this case, I believe that DPG appears to be a better-valued fund at the moment—and its distribution looks safer going forward under current conditions. Not that I believe DNP is currently considering a distribution cut, I suspect that is the last thing they would ever want to do.

If we look back at the longer-term performance comparison, DNP has easily come out on top. That said, they've become much more closely correlated in terms of underlying portfolio performance once DPG switched its investment policy. If that continues, it could make much more sense to buy up the fund that is giving similar performance but at a discount compared to a premium.

That's even if DNP is looking like its premium is relatively attractive to its own historical average, which is why I recently wrote up an update on that fund and gave it a 'Buy' rating as well. So, there is certainly some appeal there, but between the two, I would favor DPG.

data.title: The AMD Dilemma: Late To AI Amidst Cooling Demand
data.description:
Our research team is on the ground in Silicon Valley, speaking to founders and attending conferences to provide an unprecedented view of the AI market. As private investors in venture-stage companies, we bring a wealth of information that we hope will signal what is to come to the broader public market. We hope that our work will provide our followers with key information when it comes to making an investment decision. Thank you for being a reader.

AMD Overview

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) is roughly a fifth of the size of Nvidia (NVDA) when it comes to revenue. Yet the broader thesis behind AMD is that with the AI market expanding, AMD has the potential to take market share away from Nvidia. However, our team is much more cautious as we wonder whether AMD’s efforts are coming too late in the inning, as signs point to a potential slowdown in demand for AI compute. Even if AMD were to match Nvidia’s tech stack, it may be too late for the company to stay competitive, given the developer mindshare that Nvidia already has.

Too Little, Too Late

The semiconductor industry is highly cyclical and has experienced significant downturns, often in conjunction with constant and rapid technological change, wide fluctuations in supply and demand, continuous new product introductions, price erosion, and declines in general economic conditions. (AMD's 2024 10-K.)

In our view, the AI market is seeing a notable slowdown. H100 prices have collapsed as a function of muted demand and higher supply. As models and the software serving them become much more efficient and utilize a higher percentage of what was once idle GPU capacity, compute prices will naturally fall. The longer-term trend of falling prices means end-customers are less willing to commit to multi-year contracts, instead relying on spot or on-demand pricing.

The price action of Nvidia has been notably muted by earnings news as well. Their stock has been beaten down, with DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) showing the potential for open-source models to be as competitive as closed-source models.

Our read was that this was an indication of the continued closing of the gap between open-source and closed-source models, going from 3 years to about 18 months or less. This means that the lead time of top AI companies is not very long, and that their ability to continue charging higher prices for their latest models would not be sustainable. This would lead to the eventual slowdown of the AI race, which would in turn reduce the demand for top-of-the-line Nvidia chips, removing the bottleneck, and driving down prices that the picks and shovels players can charge. Add to the equation the ever-expanding presence of AMD, Apple, and hyperscaler chips like Amazon's (AMZN) Trainium and Inferentia as alternative computing platforms, and the outlook becomes more grim. (Source: Author's article on CoreWeave.)

Nvidia’s stock price has notably lacked the “pop” it experienced in the past, the last of which was in May ‘24.

The AI boom took off in November 2022 with the release of ChatGPT. We’re about 2.5 years into the boom, but AMD has yet to make a mark on the AI space. Our view is that the overall market is losing steam around AI. Note that Nvidia’s rally around Bitcoin (BTC-USD) fizzled out in about 2 years.

This isn’t to say that the AI revolution is done. There are specific use cases that are taking off, such as Cursor with its coding capability.

But it’s worth noting that coding has always been a known low-hanging use case, as seen with Github Co-pilot. The same goes for enterprise use cases like customer service and chatbots, which use a combination of large language models, or LLMs, evaluation/monitoring tools, and RAG. We view these use cases as getting better and better, but we are having a hard time seeing new use cases emerge despite a significant amount of venture dollars poured into AI.

Funding for AI and ML startups accounted for 35.7% of all VC global deal value last year, according to PitchBook data. In North America, the vertical grabbed nearly half of all VC dollars. Globally, investment in AI and ML startups increased more than 50% to $131.5 billion. (Source: Pitchbook.)

Our view is that most of this private capital went into areas that were too narrowly focused, such as mechanistic interpretability, model routing, and algorithmic-based hardware acceleration. The initial fog of war on AI made academic research coming out of universities seem significant in the upside it could provide. But we are starting to see larger players creeping into these technical innovations of startups, as seen with AWS with model routers and Alexa with consumer agents. Well-funded startups like Cursor are also implementing narrow solutions like caching and speculative decoding, modifying them to their needs, as the original developers of these techniques are realizing that their moat was insignificant and prone to creep by larger, more well-funded players.

But the core issue is that as these narrow-focused startups start going under, their demand for beefy GPUs will start fading, impacting overall demand. Additionally, hyperscalers, in an attempt to drive down cost, are creating custom chips that are specifically tailored for their own workload. These forces combined paint a tough future for AMD. The psychology driving the AI craze is starting to wear off in Silicon Valley, and we expect this to start spreading rapidly into the public markets.

Why AMD Is Behind on Software

We won’t beat a dead horse by repeating what everyone says about Nvidia: the moat is CUDA and the software around it.

Instead, we’ll give a bit more nuanced explanation of why this is an issue without getting overly technical.

Nvidia chips are designed in a particular way. The hardware design makes it so that the code running the model needs to be written to fit the architecture and design of the hardware.

Think of a piano. The black and white keys are arranged in a certain way. A musician can read the sheet music, understand which keys to press, and produce beautiful sounds. Now imagine playing that same sheet music on a guitar. The musician may understand the sheet music, but if he’s a pianist, he may not know how to effectively reproduce the music on a guitar. There may be uncertainty on what chord variation the music should be played in. The very nature of the guitar setup, with six strings and frets, means that certain techniques cannot be replicated in the same way as on the keyed piano. The musician must now figure out how to get around the shortcomings of the guitar while also learning to leverage the unique strengths that the guitar has over the piano.

Here, Nvidia is the piano and AMD is the guitar.

AMD has a software tool that allows models to be ported between Nvidia’s CUDA and AMD’s ROCm. Portability is easy, but optimization is the difficult part. Similar to a pianist playing a guitar, the model may be able to run but it won’t be maximizing the unique strengths of the AMD GPU, such as its HBM capacity, bandwidth, and the nearly 3x streaming multiprocessors that are in the AMD MI300x vs. the Nvidia H100.

The issue is that most companies building on AMD are having to invest a significant amount of effort to run AMD GPUs effectively. Some amount of custom optimization is expected, especially since different startups (foundation model providers vs. verticalized players) are using different models (audio vs. image) with different prioritized requirements (tokens per second vs. time to first token). But this becomes an issue when there’s excessive optimization that is needed, which results in companies not wanting to share their learnings and implementations.

Nvidia gets around this just by the sheer number of developers that will happily contribute to their software stack for free, as seen with Flash Attention out of Stanford and vLLM out of UC Berkeley. AMD currently lacks this community of developers, giving Nvidia a key competitive advantage and forcing developers using AMD hardware to invest significantly more resources into achieving similar performance. We see chatter about AMD’s advantage in running inference (training is much more complicated than inference), but issues still persist around the compatibility of AMD chips with existing models running on Nvidia GPUs.

We think this is a major issue that will persist. It will be an uphill battle for AMD, given that Nvidia isn’t sitting still. Nvidia still has significant scale economies and switching costs that make it nearly impossible for AMD to compete effectively.

Final Thoughts

AMD is a healthy company. They have been steadily improving from a financial perspective since their buying spree in the early 2020s. Through acquisitions of companies like Xilinx and Pensado in the past few years, AMD has found new sources of revenue in FPGA and DPUs. The post-merger integration seems to be coming along nicely, with margins improving. However, the expected main breadmaker of the company is showing signs of weakness due to the delay in developer mindshare as well as a slowing AI market. CoreWeave's muted IPO is a good indication of what may come to the picks and shovels players of the AI space. We relay a cautious message to investors considering a position in the company.

data.title: The Potential Impact Of Tariffs On Interest Rates
data.description:
Will tariffs cause inflation and thus higher interest rates? Not necessarily, says Jitania Kandhari, Deputy CIO Deputy CIO of the Solutions And Multi-Asset Group, Morgan Stanley Investment Management. She breaks down the reasons why and explains how subsidies, sanctions and security measures are reshaping the global investment environment.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: The potential impact of trade tariffs is creating wide-ranging uncertainty for the markets. So what should investors be keeping in mind as they try to navigate these choppy waters? Joining us now to discuss is Jitania Kandhari, deputy CIO of the Solutions and Multi-Asset group, co-lead portfolio manager for the Passport Equity Strategies, and head of macro and thematic research for the Emerging Markets Equity team at Morgan Stanley Investment Management.

Jitania, welcome. I want to make sure I got that out before we jump in. Thanks for joining us

Jitania Kandhari: Thank you for having me.

Anthony Okolie: And as you've heard in the markets, the US trade policy, of course, is causing a lot of uncertainty in the markets. And there's a belief that-- in the markets, that tariffs will eventually lead to higher interest rates-- higher inflation and higher interest rates. But you say that this isn't necessarily true. Walk us through your thinking.

Jitania Kandhari: Sure. So I think tariffs equals inflation is linear thinking. And market relationships are rarely linear. So, I'll tell you a couple of things. One is a lot of these tariffs can actually act as a tax on domestic production, right, because a large part of these tariff goods are intermediate goods that are further recycled back into the global trade system. Secondly, you can have retaliation by trading partners. So, all of the issues related to tariffs can actually lead to a growth slowdown and, hence, a lower inflation rather than the other way around.

That's one. The second thing is, I think there are several offsetting mechanisms. The currency weakness of the country that is tariffed is one way to offset some of that increase. You have corporates that can absorb these in their margins. Supply chains diversions can happen. And demand displacement of certain products can also occur. Just like we saw in the 2017 trade war, the RMB depreciated by nearly 13% to offset some of that tariff impact. So there are offsetting mechanisms built into the economics of the world and of trade that can lead to the-- tariffs actually not being so inflationary.

And lastly, let's see what the evidence is from [the] 2017 tariffs. And if you look at this chart, it's interesting. Canada shows that the US import price change from 2016 has been the highest for Canada. There is an issue here with oil prices because US imports a lot of oil from Canada, and oil prices have gone up significantly-- that's why that huge jump. But the most tariffed country in 2017 was China.

Anthony Okolie: That's interesting, because you see the chart there that China-- the prices, import prices, are way down.

Jitania Kandhari: Down. So despite all those tariffs against China, today the US is importing Chinese products at lower prices than 2016. Again, this is because, of course, China has excess manufacturing, which has led to price deflation, but also the currency impact with a stronger dollar and a weakening RMB that has helped offset it.

So, too much linear thinking in the markets-- and I think you really need to see the nuances here as an investor to position across different countries and themes that the market rhetoric is getting worried about.

Anthony Okolie: I've gone through your report. And you talk about-- you focus on three S's right now. You talk about subsidies, sanctions, and security. What are you seeing with these trends?

Jitania Kandhari: Yeah. So, I think subsidies, sanctions, security-- these are reshaping the political, economic, and corporate landscape today because the-- on the geopolitical side, this is-- subsidies is leading to this protectionist attitude across countries. There are sanctions that are being imposed. And there is security needed, with defense in different countries becoming a priority, with cyber attacks and that becoming a priority with corporates.

So, these three S's are really reshaping the investment environment. And I'd just like to say that, as much as people think that subsidies can really lead to onshoring and lead to clustering of industries and better growth, it's important to check the long-term impact of subsidies. And that's really debatable.

And, interesting research on the academic side has shown that subsidies actually can lead to distortion in markets and lead to reducing competition by overproduction in the subsidized industry. And as much as you can increase productivity in that industry, that increased productivity can be offset by reduced productivity in the other areas where capital is taken away from. So, it's mixed. Even though sanctions are used politically, it's interesting that only 13% of unilateral sanctions in the last five decades have actually been fruitful.

So the economic implication is not, again, so direct and linear. And we have to be really careful in how these are used. And just corporate-level, government-level, and personal-level security is-- become very important with increased threat vectors globally. And that has interesting implications for the way we're thinking of market investments as well.

Original Post

data.title: Should You Buy BB Seguridade For Its 9% Yield?
data.description:
BB Seguridade Participações S.A. (OTCPK:BBSEY) offers a very high-dividend yield of about 9%, making one of the most attractive features of its investment case. However, investors shouldn’t overlook the risks associated with investment in emerging markets and its shares don’t appear to be undervalued right now. As this is my first coverage on the company, in this article I review its business profile and most recent earnings, to see if BB Seguridade offers value for long-term investors or not.

Business Overview

BB Seguridade is a holding company formed in 2012 to own the insurance activities of Banco do Brasil (OTCPK:BDORY), which is its largest shareholder holding about 68% of its capital. The rest trades as free float on the stock exchange. BB Seguridade currently has a market value of about $14 billion and trades in the U.S. on the over-the-counter market through its ADR program.

BB Seguridade concentrates all insurance-related activities within Banco do Brasil, including insurance, savings, and brokerage insurance activities. It operates under several subsidiaries, namely Brasilseg (insurance) that generates about 70% of its earnings including brokerage, and Brasilprev (pension plans) that is responsible for some 20% of earnings, while other subsidiaries have smaller weights within the group.

By being part of Banco do Brasil’s group, BB Seguridade has access to a great distribution network through a 20-year distribution agreement that was initiated in 2013. Banco do Brasil has one of the largest distribution networks in Brazil, operating close to 4,000 banking branches throughout the country, being the main distribution channel for BB Seguridade. While the distribution agreement only has a 20-year duration, I think the most likely outcome for this agreement is to be extended, as Banco do Brasil is the main shareholder of BB Seguridade and has therefore interest in maintaining this commercial relationship over the long term.

Through its several operating subsidiaries, BB Seguridade offers several insurance and savings products, including mortgage insurance, home insurance, pension plans, or term life insurance. In Brasilseg (the main insurance subsidiary), about half of its premiums are related to rural insurance, and 21% to credit life. This means most of its premiums are related to credit products from its banking partner, showing that BB Seguridade’s business is highly dependent on Banco do Brasil’s banking volumes. The same happens in the pension plans business, where plans are mainly sold through the bancassurance channel, as well the premium bonds (a specific product of the Brazilian market) that is also distributed through the bancassurance channel.

Regarding its growth prospects, BB Seguridade is well positioned to grow from industry tailwinds, given that the insurance industry has a very good growth track record in recent years in Brazil, growing annual premiums at more than 9% during 2019-2023, but its penetration is still relatively low compared to other countries. Indeed, insurance premiums represent around 3.9% of the country’s GDP, while other emerging countries have penetration rates above 5%, compared to developed markets like the U.S. or the U.K that have penetration rates between 10-11% of GDP. This means there is still plenty of room for the insurance industry to grow in Brazil over the next few years, boding well for BB Seguridade’s growth prospects.

On top of positive industry tailwinds, the company’s strategy is to invest in digitalization and developing its technological capabilities, to improve its operating efficiency and provide a good customer service, which is also expected to be an important driver of premiums and earnings growth ahead.

Financial Overview

Regarding its financial performance, BB Seguridade has reported strong earnings in recent years, both supported by its operating performance and also due to higher investment income. Its growth track record has been quite good, as the company’s net income increased at around 16% annually in the recent past, as shown in the next graph.

In 2024, the company has maintained a positive operating momentum, given that its net income increased by 9.7% compared to the previous year to around $1.53 billion. This increase in earnings was supported by higher premiums across its several businesses, while on the other hand investment income declined compared to 2023.

Regarding its top-line, overall written premiums increased by 2.2% YoY to more than $3 billion, supported by strong growth in credit life and rural lien insurance, which basically protects the asset given as collateral for a rural loan. The company also reported increased activity related to digital channels, which is an important part of its business strategy, as the company invested close to $95 million in IT infrastructure, cybersecurity, and new products and digital solutions. On the other hand, its investment income dropped by 17% YoY to $237 million, representing about 16.5% of its annual net income (vs. 21% in 2023) due to reductions in the Selic rate and inflationary impacts on liabilities.

Regarding new products, BB Seguridade has launched livestock lien insurance and simplified personal protection policies during 2024, diversifying even further its net premiums written mix and being another source of top-line growth. Regarding simplified personal protection insurance, this is a life insurance product with simplified coverage and more affordable prices, being important to gain new customers and eventually cross-sell more complex products in the future. In 2024, BB Seguridade was able to reach 85,000 policies related to this new insurance coverage, which can be considered a positive result, and can be an important source of premiums growth over the coming years.

Regarding its claims, the loss ratio reached a new historical low last year, which is supported by BB Seguridade’s strict underwriting criteria and also due to its reinsurance strategy. Its loss ratio was only 23.7% in 2024 (vs. 26.7% in 2023), resulting on a higher underwriting profit for the year, which was an important support to offset lower investment income, leading to an adjusted net income of $1.43 billion in 2024 (+6% YoY).

As the company has a good capital position, with all subsidiaries well above the regulatory requirements, BB Seguridade decided to distribute almost all of its earnings to shareholders, both through dividends and share buybacks. Indeed, its dividend distribution amounted to $1.25 billion and performed share repurchases of $210 million, leading to a payout ratio of 95% based on reported profit. This shows that the company is clearly committed to provide an attractive shareholder remuneration policy over the long term, being one of the most attractive features of its investment case. At its current share price, BB Seguridade is currently offering a dividend yield of about 9%, being quite attractive to income-oriented investors.

Regarding its guidance for 2025, the company expects net written premiums to grow between 2-7% compared to the previous year, supported by insurance lines that aren’t related to credit, such as life and residential insurance. The pension plan reserves are expected to increase between 12-16% compared to 2024, and its non-interest operating result should grow between 3-8% in 2025. This positive operating performance expected for 2025 is clearly supportive of dividend growth ahead, a trend that according to analysts’ estimates is expected to be maintained over the coming years. This means its high-dividend yield seems to be sustainable and is not a reflection of potential issues with its business model, being therefore a good opportunity for investors.

Regarding BB Seguridade’s valuation, its shares are currently trading at 8.2x book value, which is a relatively high valuation multiple in the insurance sector, as most companies in the P&C segment trade between 2-4x book value. While at first glance this may mean its shares are overvalued, investors should consider that BB Seguridade reports a very high return on equity (ROE) ratio, that was close to 90% in 2024. This is justified by high interest rates in Brazil, but also by its business model that is highly exposed to the banking distribution of Banco do Brasil, leading to a more efficient operation than if it would operate as a stand-alone company. This results in high levels of profitability, justifying its premium valuation to peers. Having said that, its average price-to-book value multiple has been around 7.1x over the past five years, thus BB Seguridade doesn’t seem to be undervalued right now.

Moreover, investors should also consider that the company operates in an emerging market with some political risk and less macroeconomic stability than developed countries, making it more risky than other insurance companies that are more exposed to the U.S. or other developed countries, which may justify a lower valuation over the long term compared to companies that report the same level of profitability but operate in other regions.

Conclusion

BB Seguridade is an interesting insurance company in Brazil, having a competitive advantage by being part of Banco do Brasil group and having access to its large distribution network in the country. Despite that, its financial performance in recent quarters can be considered positive but not impressive, and its shares are trading at a relatively high valuation. In my opinion, its high-dividend yield is the main reason to consider its shares in the short term, but investors should consider that Brazil’s macroeconomic risk is higher than compared to developed countries and the company’s dividend policy is set in local currency, making ADR investors also exposed to currency risk. Given this background, I don’t think BB Seguridade is compelling enough right now for long-term investors despite its high-dividend yield, and investors should wait for a pullback before buying its shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Exxon Mobil: Keep Your Expectations Low In 2025
data.description:
The past year has confirmed that Exxon Mobil's (NYSE:XOM) shareholders should no longer expect stellar returns.

That's exactly what I warned about last April, when I laid out my reasoning why XOM is likely to face short-term challenges. Fast forward 12 months to today and XOM's share price is essentially flat.

Periods such as 2020, when XOM was trading at a significant discount and expected returns were very high, are quite rare for an Oil & Gas company the size of Exxon. That is why, it is reasonable to expect that the performance gap with the S&P 500 will remain, with the occasional narrowing-down when investors' sentiment catches up to reality (as it was following my initial buy rating in 2020).

For investors who are focusing on near-term performance, however, it appears as if XOM and Energy companies more broadly might be a refuge in 2025. The Energy sector takes the top spot on a year-to-date basis with a positive 9% return in just three months.

This performance runs counterintuitive to the sector-rotation narrative where investors flock to lower risk areas, such as consumer staples and utilities. As we can see on the graph below XOM's performance detached from that of the S&P 500 in early March and since then the two have gone in opposite directions.

This dynamic was almost entirely driven by the bottoming of oil prices in early March and investors adjusting their short-term expectations. Unfortunately, support for increased drilling for petroleum and gas is likely to put a lid on prices for the time being.

On top of that we have the increased risk of a recession with the recent PMI report showing more signs of contraction.

Based on all that, I wouldn't bet on XOM and the Energy sector more broadly continuing to outperform the equity market by a very wide margin. With that in mind, let's remain focused on Exxon's attractiveness as a long-term investment and whether or not now is a good time to increase positions.

Zooming Out

A good starting point when looking at XOM's attractiveness on a long-term basis is of course the stock's dividend yield.

Back in September of 2020, when I first rated the stock as a buy, XOM had a dividend yield that was approaching 10%. As of today, the rate has fallen to just slightly above 3%, while at the same the time the yield on U.S. Treasuries is above 4%.

We see a similar story when looking at Exxon's free cash flow yield, which is now back to the historical norm of around 6%.

Given the cyclical nature of the industry, we should also consider the level of capital expenditure as a share of the annual depreciation expense in order to adjust for any fluctuations. As of the end of fiscal year 2024, Exxon's annual capex remains roughly in-line with the depreciation expense which is a clear signal that the company is not aggressively expanding (yet).

Partially responsible for this relatively low capex to depreciation ratio was the deal with Pioneer Natural Resources which will be a major tailwind for production growth for years to come. On top of that, Exxon's management is committed to growing production over the coming years and with that it is reasonable to expect a more notable increase in capital expenditure.

By 2030, at a 2024 dollar real Brent price of $65 per barrel, a real Henry Hub price of $3 per mmbtu, and a real TTF price of $6.50 per mmbtu, the company plans to deliver an additional $9 billion in Upstream annual earnings potential – more than 50% higher than in 2024.

With the Pioneer acquisition, the company reached its target of having more than 50% of its total Upstream production from advantaged assets (Permian, Guyana, and LNG) three years earlier than planned. By 2030, more than 60% of the company’s production is expected to come from these advantaged assets, which are expected to grow by an additional 1.2 million oil-equivalent barrels per day (Moebd) during that period. Total Upstream production is expected to reach 5.4 Moebd by 2030 (...)

Source: Exxon Mobil Website

In fiscal year 2024 capital expenditure stood at round $24.3bn, which is expected to increase in 2025 and then again in the following year.

This coming year in 2025, we said we expect cash capex to be between $27 billion and $29 billion. And then, between 2026 and 2030, that number is $28 billion to $33 billion.

Source: Exxon Mobil Q4 2024 Earnings Transcript

This is a positive sign for long-term investors, albeit the rate of return on these future projects is far less certain in an environment that is likely to be more supportive to drilling activities. And even if we assume that XOM would achieve high returns on its future investments, there is no reason to rush and buy the stock at this point in time.

What's In Store For 2025?

While Exxon is well-positioned to continue to reward shareholders over the long-run, the prospects for the rest of 2025 appear limited.

On a quarterly basis, upstream earnings are roughly in-line with current oil prices and it seems that investors should not anticipate any meaningful improvement based on prices.

As we saw above, the market is still repricing the stock based on its near-term expectations about the oil market, however, prices remain below their prior year highs. Thus, upstream margins are likely to contract on year-on-year basis through the rest of 2025, unless we see a more radical shift in pricing within the oil market.

With the exception of natural gas prices, which are above their historical averages, all areas of XOM's business remain under pressure. Refining and chemical margins are still below their long-term averages and Q4 2024 results did not suggest that this is about to change anytime soon.

Maintenance outlook for Q1 2025 has improved within the upstream segment, but this is likely to be offset by higher scheduled maintenance within Energy, Chemical and Specialty Products divisions.

When we add the increased probability of a recession in the U.S. in 2025 and it's hard to conceive a scenario where XOM significantly outperforms the equity market through the rest of 2025.

Conclusion

I stand firmly behind my initial investment thesis on Exxon Mobil which gravitates around the company's competitive positioning and long-term supportive industry tailwinds. Having said that, however, it seems highly unlikely that XOM's share price will deliver outstanding results in 2025. On top of the increasing short-term macroeconomic risks, XOM is also now priced much closer to its fair value. Therefore, I retain my Hold rating for the time being and will be looking for more attractive entry points.

data.title: Hospitals Experience FOMO In Adopting Delcath's PHP Treatment
data.description:
Another Sales Beat

DelcathSystems (NASDAQ:DCTH) has been pre-announcing sales results recently. That created a long lag between the sales announcement and the earnings report since its Q4. It makes the most sense for me to do the updates after the report and call, since that’s when I can add the most color. When Delcath pre-announced Q4 sales in mid-January, they beat estimates for $12.42 million, coming in at $15.1 million.

Delcath has been adding new hospitals at a slower than expected rate, but usage has been driving sales beats. That’s the ideal method of beating estimates because the hospitals will eventually all be activated since they have shown interest. It would have been extremely short-sighted to sell the stock because of worries over fewer procedures during the holiday season.

As a result of this report, 2025 sales estimates increased to $83.53 million. They are up 7.6% in the past month and 14.3% in the past 3 months. Estimate revisions are the main driver of stocks. As I will get to later, I think this sales estimate is still beatable. In the past month, EPS estimates for 2026 and 2027 increased 80.8% and 70.5%.

The stock briefly cratered after this report, which was on March 6th, due to the dire bear market in Biotech/MedTech. Delcath skirts the line between a risky medical device stock just beginning commercialization and a strong business with steady profits, since it just had its first quarter of profitability. The former have felt the most pain in the past 5 months. Clinical stage Biotech/MedTech stocks have been decimated.

Profits Are Here After Decades Of Losses

In Q4, Delcath reported adjusted EBITDA of $4.6 million. If you take out stock-based compensation, adjusted EBITDA was $3 million. There was a large $6.7 million non-cash fair value warrant adjustment that I have no problem taking out. The firm only burned $1 million in cash and has no debt. It has $53.2 million in cash now. If the stock stays above $10 in May, the firm will get a final $17 million in cash from exercised warrants. These are the last ones, putting fully diluted shares outstanding at 43.8 million.

We must take a step back in awe of the current situation. After decades of losses and a stock price that’s literally down 100% due to dilution, Delcath now has an FDA approved drug/device, a strong balance sheet, and can be profitable whenever it wants. The only way it will have quarters of losses in 2025 is if it chooses to spend money aggressively on its 2 clinical trials. It has more than enough cash to do so. Getting new FDA approval of new indications as quickly as possible is well worth small losses.

I am grateful I have been able to capture most of the gains without any of the volatility prior to the FDA approval of HEPZATO KIT, especially during the biotech bear market which has been ongoing for over 4 years. The most amazing stat of 2024 is that SG&A only increased from $22.1 million to $29.6 million. This tiny increase drove sales up from $2.1 million to $37.2 million. The model works because gross margins are 86% and operating costs are minimal due to the need for only ~40 hospitals in America. In 2024, SG&A is only expected to rise 30-40%, with sales potentially increasing 135.6% as I will get to in my projection section.

SG&A Spending Will Be Well Worth It

Delcath’s SG&A spending will go towards increasing the commercial structure from 4 to 6 regions in the US, which will activate hospitals quicker. Each region has a liver directed therapy manager, an oncology manager, and a clinical manager. The liver directed therapy manager is the main person who will drive hospital adoption through quick approvals, training, and managing patients. Clinical specialists improve patient outcomes and safety (execution of the procedure).

The referral network won’t be as big as originally thought because there will be more centers than expected (patients less willing to travel). Still, the oncology manager’s goal of increasing referrals is important. It will drive procedure growth in the centers that have the most experience. Dr. Jonathan Zager from Moffitt Cancer Center tweeted that he hopes to more than surpass 50 procedures in 2024. Remember, the average center, which includes many newer ones, is slightly below 2 per month, which is about 22-23 for the year (Moffitt is more than double that). Finally, the firm will also hire some people to help with coding and claim submissions. Each hire is well worth the return on investment.

European Expansion & Reimbursement Struggles

Interestingly, Delcath sold about as many units in Europe as it did in America. The reimbursement differential is stark. In 2024, CHEMOSAT in Europe only had $4.9 million in sales compared to $32.3 million from HEPZATO KIT in America (remember, they are the same device).

Quick back of the envelope math suggests CHEMOSAT’s revenue per procedure was about $27,724 in Europe. With costs per procedure about $16,831, it’s operating at around $11k gross profit per procedure in Europe. In this back of the envelope calculation, I assumed HEPZATO KIT & CHEMOSAT cost the same to produce, which might be slightly off because the melphalan is sold separately in Europe, which might lower Delcath’s costs there. Delcath also has much fewer sales reps in Europe than in America because of its lower gross profitability, which helps its operating profitability. The goal has been to keep Europe around breakeven.

CHEMOSAT’s sales were up 133.3% in 2024 which was entirely driven by 137% volume growth. There were no sales boosts from better reimbursement. It’s possible this growth rate ratio (sales vs. volumes) was slightly impacted by usage in studies that don’t generate sales, but that’s not enough to impact how we see pricing. German sales were up 75%; other markets such as Turkey and the UK doubled in 2024. Germany is the only country reimbursing CHEMOSAT. In the Netherlands, hospitals have been taking it out of their own budgets. In the UK, it’s only private pay. We would need to see one of these two countries grant reimbursement to generate sales growth above volume growth. More importantly, we need to see reimbursement for better margins. The main current use case for Europe is generating data from studies.

Let’s look at the nitty-gritty details on reimbursement in Europe. Firstly, in December 2024 the National Institute for Health & Care Excellence (NICE) in the UK recommended KIMMTRAK (systemic treatment for mUM in HLA positive patients) after initially giving it a negative decision in May 2023.

In April 2021, NICE revised its guidance for CHEMOSAT from a research only designation to a Special Arrangements designation. However, NHS (National Health Service) England said it won’t be reimbursed. NHS blamed what NICE described as "serious complications" for its decision not to reimburse PHP. However, they are open to changing that decision and conducting more reviews if the evidence for the treatment changes. Remember, the TAM in the UK is 500-600 patients per year.

On the other hand, ever since 2016 CHEMOSAT has been reimbursed in Germany through a combination of a Diagnostic Related Group (DRG) code and an additional fee called a Zusatzentgelt.

The CHEMOSAT website says there are over 22 centers in Europe doing the procedure. I counted the centers listed. These are the totals from each country: 1 in Sweden, 1 distributor in Turkey, 1 in Italy, 1 in the Netherlands, 4 in UK, 10 in Germany. According to management, the firm plans to expand into France, Italy, and Spain. The biggest issue here is reimbursement, not usage or expanding hospitals. By growing in new countries, it might add more shots on goal for potential reimbursement.

Once one domino falls, more should follow. Without reimbursement, the only other way to improve margins is to increase the volume of procedures in the other more common indications like mCRC (metastatic colorectal cancer), mBC (metastatic breast cancer), and ICC (intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma).

For now, European expansion adds to the potential list of hospitals Delcath can pick from for its two Phase 2 clinical trials. Plus, we have the CHOPIN & SCANDIUM combination trials, which can further cement PHP (percutaneous hepatic perfusion (HEPZATO KIT/CHEMOSAT)) as the standard of care in mUM (metastatic uveal melanoma). There might be more investigator-initiated trials in Europe. I’ve seen enough data on mUM from the FOCUS trial. We’ll get even more data from the two combination trials’ control arms.

I’d like to see more basket trials which include other tumor types. That will derisk the results from the two Phase 2 trials. Remember, because CHEMOSAT is approved as a device in Europe, it has been allowed to treat various tumor types without additional trials. Over 10 studies were published in 2024 as I have reviewed in previous articles.

Tortuous, But Steady Expansion In The US

Delcath’s CEO Gerard Michel described adding new US hospitals as an "almost torturous process." This is counteracted by the fear of missing out (FOMO) hospitals that don’t have HEPZATO KIT are feeling when they see competing hospitals adopt it with great success. Unlike in Europe, HEPZATO KIT is getting reimbursed and at a premium price. Due to inflation, the price increased 2.7% to $187.5k per treatment in 2025 (4.1 treatments per patient). I didn’t include inflation increasing prices in my original valuation prediction, so that’s a nice bonus.

Delcath ended 2024 with 14 hospitals doing procedures in the US. It has since added another 3 in Q1, as you can see below. Hospitals want to be activated, but the process has continued to go slower than expected. It’s a matter of getting through the formularies and the Value Analysis Committees. It’s possible that management has become more conservative in response to being too optimistic in 2024.

There are currently 7 hospitals accepting referrals, but not yet doing procedures. These 7 more need to become activated along with another 6 to meet the firm’s target of 30 by year end. That shouldn’t be a problem because management is having conversations with 20+ additional centers. Because the hires that I mentioned will occur in the middle of this year, center activation will be back-end weighted. Guidance was for 6 in 1H and 10 in 2H.

Usage has been the backbone of sales beats because new centers keep taking longer to activate. Usage per center was slightly below 2 per month in Q4. Management expects that rate to continue in 2025, but I think that’s a little too conservative. The average will increase once centers become more used to the procedure and have patients that require the full 6 doses. Usually, there is a gap after the first couple procedures before the centers are confident in their workflow and its reimbursement.

With some of the procedures around Christmas being pushed into Q1 and only 3 centers being activated in the quarter, we might see a slightly higher average number of treatments per center. I’m confident higher usage will pull through starting in Q2, but I hesitate slightly in getting too optimistic on Q1 since at the time of the call, the quarter was mostly over. It’s hard to guide conservatively with only 25 days left in the quarter, but maybe they did anyway. Treatments per center will slightly fall in 2H if another 10 are added. 2026’s growth will mostly come from more usage per center since only 10 more will be added. PHP might get an additional tailwind that year from the CHOPIN readout this fall.

Biggest Learnings From The Call

For investors like me who spend a lot of time reading about Delcath, there wasn’t that much new in this report/call. That’s why I was so surprised by the stock’s volatility. It’s clearly more of a function of the overall market than the business. With that being said, there were two incremental points reinforced (1 good & 1 bad).

The bad news is it seems unlikely that Delcath will be moving into the ICC indication soon after mUM. I was hopeful that Delcath could smooth over the growth lull in between mUM reaching saturation and mCRC/mBC gaining adoption following their clinical trials. Back in my first article, I mentioned management discussed with me in an email that the firm could get ICC added to the NCCN (National Comprehensive Cancer Network) guidelines with existing publications or a small trial. Then, the plan was to get reimbursement via peer-reviewed publications including case studies and single arm basket trials with multiple tumor types. Now getting ICC added to the NCCN guidelines isn’t a priority (based on email correspondence).

Delcath is telling centers not to do procedures off label because there won’t be payer coverage. Specifically, Gerard said on the call that he wouldn’t bake in sales from off label usage anytime in the next year or so. He also mentioned ICC and mPC (metastatic pancreatic cancer) as additional tumor types that it would make sense to expand to. I get the feeling it’s going to be harder to get ICC reimbursed than we initially thought. It’s not that ICC is being abandoned. I just think it’s very unlikely to have a commercial impact in 2026; it might not even in 2027. It could need a trial which would put it behind the mCRC and mBC timelines.

The good news is PHP faces lower competition risk than some may have expected. I’ll get into how Delcath framed competition in their slide later on. The point here is PHP is already dealing with IDEAYA’s (IDYA) Darovasertib and Crizotinib combination therapy due to its clinical trials. Some worried there would be a major difference in PHP usage between before and after IDEAYA’s approval. However, because the treatment population is so small, trials give us a strong early reading on how the treatment landscape will play out. Remember, there are only about 800 mUM patients annually in the US eligible for PHP. Therefore, IDEAYA’s 230 patient enrollment in HLA negative patients is having a substantial impact already. The approval isn’t going to cause a drastic change.

These aren’t competitive treatments. All stakeholders are working together to formulate the correct series of treatments because none are a cure. The goal is to create a successful protocol to extend patients’ lives as long as possible. An approval could be bullish for Delcath because in the trials patients need to be treatment naïve. This means patients can’t get PHP first or in the midst of IDEAYA’s treatment. With that being said, the order isn’t too important because patients almost always progress.

Because it takes 6-8 weeks to get treated with PHP due to prior authorization, mapping the patient, and evaluations, the patients get put on KIMMTRAK or Ipi/Nivo (ipilimumab/nivolumab) first.
